items.headline: The Day I Quit My Business
items.angle: Frame the 'Build Online, Live Offline' philosophy not as a lifestyle choice, but as a necessary survival tactic born from a specific moment of intense burnout. Tell the story of the breaking point that forced you to reject the 'hustle' model and build the systems that became FieldWork.
hooks: I was on a boat in the Maldives when I realized I had to shut down my business.
The day I hit my biggest revenue month was the day I decided to burn it all down.
My business was trapping me in paradise. Here's how I escaped.
I had two choices: keep scaling the business or actually start living my life.
I built a successful business that was costing me the one thing I couldn't get back.
talking_points: It was a Tuesday in October. I was on a dive boat in the Maldives, supposedly living the dream, but I was glued to my phone, putting out fires back home.
I had a huge realization in front of me. I had to rebuild my entire business model or risk losing the very freedom I started it for in the first place.
I had built a successful agency, a business that looked great on paper. But I was the central bottleneck.
Every client email, every project, every problem ran directly through me. I had built a prison of my own design, trading my time for dollars.
And faced with that, I had two choices. I could lean into the hustle, keep grinding 16 hour days and pretend this burnout was the price of success.
Or I could let go of the ego, remove myself as the bottleneck, and build something that could actually run without me.
And I did. I built the systems, the automations, the team. I built a business that funds my life instead of consuming it.
I've spent more time in the field, on expeditions, and with family in the last two years than in the five before that combined.
I can tell you two things. It would not have happened if I kept glorifying the hustle, and it would not have happened without committing to building real systems.
If you feel trapped by the business you built, I want to send you my free guide on the 3 systems that bought my time back. Just comment 'SYSTEMS' below and I'll send it over.

items.headline: The Original Version of You
items.angle: Frame the entrepreneurial journey as a path that starts with freedom but gets lost in 'hustle culture' expectations. Position FieldWork as the way to reclaim that original vision of a life lived on your own terms, not just a business that owns you.
hooks: This was me before I was told success meant being online 24/7.
Remember when your business was supposed to give you more freedom?
This was the goal before the grind became the entire point.
This was me before my business started owning my calendar.
I started a business to live more, not to live online.
talking_points: This was me before I was told success meant being online 24/7.
Before 'rise and grind' was the only strategy. Before you had to post five times a day to be 'relevant'.
It was before a full calendar started feeling more productive than a full life.
At some point, we start calling this 'scaling' or 'the hustle'.
But what really happens is the original reason we started gets buried.
The instinct to build a business that serves your life, not the other way around. The desire to work from anywhere, so you can actually be anywhere you want.
That part of you doesn't just disappear. It just gets buried under notifications and other people's expectations.
So when you see someone logging off at 2 PM to go surfing, or taking a month to travel with their family, that feeling you get isn't jealousy.
It's a reminder.

The original reason you started all this is still there, waiting for you to build the systems that make it possible. I put together a guide on how we do it. Just comment 'FieldWork' and I'll send it to you.
